nativeRange.habitat: Clear and blackwater streams and tributaries with sandy substrates, often influenced by cool Andean snowmelt runoff
nativeRange.biotope: Rio Ucayali basin (Aquas River), upper Amazon drainage

diet.notes: Readily accepts sinking pellets and wafers as a staple. Benefits from regular supplementation with frozen or live foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, and brine shrimp nauplii.

imageCandidates.notes: Chrischang; 1600x1200. Group of four in a planted tank, foreground fish sharp and vibrant, clear black eye mask, dorsal saddle and black caudal base; best represents the schooling species in the hobby.
PRODornenwolf; 3561x2256. Tack-sharp side profile of a healthy adult showing all diagnostic panda markings and barbels crisply; second fish softly blurred behind.
Haplochromis; 1599x1066. Sharp pair foraging under driftwood, gold sheen, clean panda pattern; adds a two-fish angle for variety.

summary: A small, peaceful schooling catfish from cool Andean streams in Peru, recognized by its white body and distinctive black panda-style eye mask. Prefers cooler water than most Corydoras.
careNotes: The Panda Cory prefers cooler water (20–25°C) than many popular tropical Corydoras — notably cooler than C. sterbai (which thrives at 25–30°C). This cooler preference should be weighed carefully when choosing tankmates, as pairing it with warm-water species can mean a compromise temperature that suits neither fish well. A minimum school of 6 is required, and the species tends to be more active and confident in larger groups. A soft sand substrate at least 3 cm deep is important to protect the delicate barbels, which are prone to erosion on coarse gravel or in conditions where detritus accumulates. Regular water changes and substrate vacuuming are strongly advised, as the species is sensitive to poor water quality. Like all Corydoras, the Panda Cory is sensitive to copper and should never be kept in a system treated with copper-based medications. It is often confused with or sold mixed with the Adolfo's Cory (C. adolfoi), which can be distinguished by an orange blaze positioned just behind the eye mask — verifying the species at point of purchase is worthwhile. The species was reclassified by taxonomists as Hoplisoma panda, though the older name Corydoras panda remains the name most widely used in the aquarium trade. The distinctive panda pattern — large black eye patches, a black dorsal saddle, and a black caudal base — makes visual identification straightforward once the difference from C. adolfoi is understood. Adults in captivity may take several months to years before they are ready to condition and spawn. Related species worth comparing include [corydoras-aeneus] (warmer, hardier, and more widely available), [corydoras-sterbai] (a warm-water alternative), and [corydoras-julii] (similar spotted patterning).
breedingNotes: Seasonal spawner. Conditioning with frequent live/frozen food feedings and a slight temperature drop (simulating Andean wet season) tends to trigger spawning. Eggs are typically laid on tank glass or broad plant leaves. Adults have been known to consume eggs if not removed promptly. Eggs generally hatch in 3–5 days at target temperature. Fry are small and accept microworm and brine shrimp nauplii as first foods. A ratio of approximately 2 males per female is commonly recommended. Sexual maturity may take a year or more.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided water parameters, size, tank dimensions, diet, native range, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Callichthyidae, order Siluriformes), size (3.8 cm SL), temperature range (20–25°C), and native range (upper Amazon, Peru). FishBase now lists the accepted name as Hoplisoma panda; Corydoras panda retained as trade/common synonym. Size discrepancy resolved: FishBase 3.8 cm is standard length (no caudal fin), SeriouslyFish 5 cm is total length — both correct for their measurement convention.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: General Corydoras care guide; confirms group size minimum of 6, sinking food preference, and soft substrate requirement. No panda-specific parameter page found on this source.
